one_line_takeaway: O3 achieves retained-data-free continual LLM unlearning by pairing an orthogonally-regularized shared LoRA (to stop successive unlearning requests from interfering) with an unsupervised OOD detector that soft-loads that LoRA only when the input resembles unlearned knowledge, beating seven baselines that fail even with full retained data.
key_finding: O3 attains the best balance of unlearning effectiveness and utility preservation across all three tasks WITHOUT using any retained data, whereas the seven baselines are given full retained data and still fail: they either fail to unlearn (EUL leaves TOFU S.U. at 80.1, NPO leaves CLINC150 S.U. at 99.9) or unlearn but destroy utility (GradDif/SOGD collapse CLINC150 MRPC to ~2.9-12.9). Baselines' utility also degrades sharply once retained data drops below ~20% (250-1000 samples), which the paper argues is the common real-world regime. O3's OOD detector is central and effective: the full design reaches near-ceiling AUROC (e.g. 99.5-100 on ScienceQA domains, 92.5-99.2 on TOFU), beating MDF/Agg and every ablation.
core_contribution: The paper proposes O3, a framework for CONTINUAL LLM unlearning that removes the influence of undesired data (private, toxic, illegal, copyrighted, or unsafe knowledge) across a stream of sequentially arriving unlearning requests while preserving model utility, and crucially WITHOUT access to any retained dataset. O3 has two components authored by this paper (not cited prior work): (1) an Orthogonal-regularized LoRA that unlearns via preference optimization (random/reject labels) and adds an orthogonal regularization loss L_Orth = ||(A^{t-1})^T A^t||^2 to disentangle the LoRA column space of successive requests so earlier unlearning is not overwritten; and (2) an unsupervised Out-Of-Distribution detector that treats the unlearning set as in-distribution and, at inference, decides whether and how strongly to load the unlearning LoRA (soft-weighted inference: h' = Wh + w(x)*ABh). The OOD detector is trained with a novel contrastive entropy loss (L_CEL, applied per-layer, supplemented with an MLM loss) and scored with a 'glocal-aware' mechanism combining per-layer Mahalanobis distance (global Gaussian) and max instance-wise cosine similarity (local), aggregated into a vector fed to a one-class SVM whose hypersphere boundary yields the soft weight.
threat_model: The setting is real-world LLM data governance: a model provider must, on request, scrub the influence of specific undesired data (privacy information, copyrighted/authorized-expired data, toxic content, or dangerous knowledge such as WMDP unsafe behaviors) from a deployed LLM. Two realistic constraints define the threat/operational model: (i) unlearning requests arrive CONTINUALLY, so naive sequential unlearning accumulates catastrophic utility loss; and (ii) the original training / retained data is often inaccessible due to privacy, copyright, and expired authorization, so methods that lean on a large retained set to protect utility are unrealistic. The paper also considers adversaries that try to defeat unlearning: appendices report robustness against targeted relearning attacks (F.9) and adversarial inputs crafted to bypass the OOD unlearning-knowledge detector (G.2), plus membership-inference-attack evaluation (F.6).
limitations: Evaluated on a single target model (LLaMA2-7b) with a single fixed LoRA rank (8), so scaling to larger/other architectures is untested in the main text. The design's cost grows with the number of past requests T: at inference each input is forwarded through ALL T OOD-detector backbones {F^1..F^T} to compute per-request soft weights, and per-request score vectors, LoRA, and OCSVM must be stored; the reported '+5.6% inference overhead' is a snapshot, not an asymptotic bound. Utility on the retained distribution is slightly below the untouched base model. The whole approach hinges on the unlearning data being distributionally separable by the OOD detector; the honesty/robustness of that assumption under adaptive adversaries is only addressed in appendices (relearning attacks F.9, detector bypass G.2). The comparison is somewhat asymmetric by construction (baselines adapted to a continual setting they were not designed for), though this is the paper's explicitly motivated regime.

datasets_used: ScienceQA (QA; 6,508 train / 2,224 test text-only samples; unlearning domains biology->physics->chemistry->economics)
OpenbookQA (QA utility U.2; 4,957 train / 500 val / 500 test)
TOFU (Fictitious Knowledge Generation; forget01/forget05/forget10 as three continual requests; +400-sample retained set; Real-Authors and World-Facts utility sets)
CLINC150 (Intent Classification; 150 classes/5 domains; unlearning domains work->travel->home)
quantitative_results: Trainable parameters: O3 ~20M via LoRA vs baselines' full 6,758M LLM (<3%)
Training data: O3 uses ~half the baselines' data (ScienceQA 2,427 vs 4,854; TOFU 640 vs 1,280; CLINC150 1,200 vs 2,400) because it needs no retained set
Inference compute overhead only 5.6% above baselines (Appendix F.1)

TOFU request-3 (Table 2): O3 S.U.=15.5, D.U.=19.7 (strong unlearning) with R.D.=84.9, R.A.=88.8, W.F.=86.1 (near Base R.D.=85.8/R.A.=89.0/W.F.=86.3); EUL fails to unlearn (S.U.=80.1); SOPO weaker unlearning (S.U.=34.5)

CLINC150 request-3 (Table 3): O3 S.U.=40.6, D.U.=42.4, R.D.=97.8, MRPC=86.6, RTE=89.0; NPO fails to unlearn (S.U.=99.9); SOGD/GradDif unlearn but wreck utility (SOGD MRPC=2.9; GradDif S.U.=0 but MRPC=12.9)
OOD detector AUROC (Table 4, 'Ours full' best): ScienceQA domains 99.4-100; TOFU-forget01 R.D./R.A./W.F.=97.8/99.0/99.2, beating MDF (90.5/96.6/97.6) and Agg (94.4/98.0/98.0)

Orthogonal-loss ablation (Table 5): lambda=0 gives ScienceQA S.U.=15.3 vs lambda=0.1 S.U.=9.3 and CLINC150 R.D. jumps from 21.8 (lambda=0) to strong values with L_Orth on, confirming L_Orth is necessary; lambda=0.1 chosen
baselines_compared: OOD-detector baselines: MDF, Agg; ablations Ours w/ SimCLR, Ours w/ MoCo, Ours w/o dCos, Ours w/o dMaha, Ours w/ last layer
